m_Item1.QuestionText: Statement: 'We need to take drastic measures to control the alarming rate of pollution in the city,' says the environmental scientist on a television panel. Assumption: The current measures being taken are insufficient to control pollution.

Options.OptionText: Yes, the scientist believes current measures are enough.
No, the statement does not imply any assumptions.
Yes, the statement implies current measures are insufficient.
No, the statement suggests other issues are causing pollution.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assertion made by the environmental scientist suggests that because of the 'alarming rate of pollution,' the existing efforts to manage or reduce pollution are inadequate, hence requiring more drastic actions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The city council has proposed a new law banning single-use plastics.
Assumptions:
I. The new law will help reduce plastic pollution.
II. Everyone in the city will comply with the new law.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit
Only assumption II is implicit
Either I or II is implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit
Both I and II are implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The company is hiring more customer service representatives.
Assumptions:
I. The company has seen an increase in customer complaints.
II. The new customer service representatives will be able to handle all customer inquiries.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit
Only assumption II is implicit
Either I or II is implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit
Both I and II are implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: A college degree is necessary to secure a good job.
Assumptions:
I. All good jobs require a college degree.
II. Having a college degree increases the chances of securing a good job.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit
Only assumption II is implicit
Either I or II is implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit
Both I and II are implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Everybody loves reading Gita.
Assumptions:
(I)   Gita is the only spiritual material.
(II) Not a single person loves reading any other material.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit
Only assumption II is implicit
Either I or II is implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit
Both I and II are implicit

m_Item1.Explanation: According to the above-given case, by reading the assumptions neither (I) nor (II) can be reasonably drawn from the given statement.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Do not throw the garbage outside the dustbin.
Assumptions:
(I)   It is possible to throw the garbage outside the dustbin.
(II) Such warning will have some effect.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit
Only assumption II is implicit
Either I or II is implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit
Both I and II are implicit

m_Item1.Explanation:
If it was not possible to throw the garbage outside the dustbin, there would be no need for the notice. Hence,(I) is implicitly true.
Likewise, the notice would not be placed if the warning would have no impact. Hence, (II) is also implicit.
So, the correct answer is E.

m_Item1.QuestionText:

I. Films are the only media of entertainment.
II. People do not enjoy films.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: Shivam wrote to his brother at Delhi to collect personally the application form for the research degree in Microbiology from the University.
Assumptions:
1. It may possible that the University will not accept the application forms to a person other than the prospective student.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The company will be closed on Sundays.

II. The company is losing money by staying open on Sundays.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit
Only assumption II is implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: Regular exercise is essential for good health.
Assumptions:
I . People who do not exercise regularly will have poor health.

Options.OptionText: Only assumption I is implicit

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The government has announced a significant cut in the prices of eco-friendly vehicles.
Assumption: People are interested in buying eco-friendly vehicles.

Options.OptionText: Only to incentivize automakers.
To reduce traffic congestion.
To promote environmental conservation.
People's interest in eco-friendly vehicles is high.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that people are interested in eco-friendly benefits; otherwise, price cuts would not effectively promote these vehicles.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The corporation has decided to reduce the workforce to reduce its cost.
Assumption: Reducing workforce is an effective method to cut costs.

Options.OptionText: The corporation is expanding operations.
The corporation is not concerned about employee welfare.
Reducing workforce directly reduces labor costs.
Competitors are reducing costs similarly.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that reducing the workforce will lead to reduced costs, as employees' salaries and benefits constitute a significant part of operational costs.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The university announced that it would organize a job fair on campus.
Assumption: The university has collaborations with companies.

Options.OptionText: Students are interested in job placements.
Alumni will get better job opportunities.
Companies are willing to recruit from the university.
Students prefer internships over jobs.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that companies are interested in recruiting from the university, which is why a job fair is being organized.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The manager asked the team to accelerate the project timeline.
Assumption: The team has the capacity to work faster.

Options.OptionText: The project is of low importance.
The team is not currently working at maximum efficiency.
The manager wants to build team morale.
The project resources are unlimited.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that the team can work faster and is not currently operating at peak efficiency, prompting the manager's direction.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: Experts suggest regular exercise to maintain good health.
Assumption: People aim to maintain good health.

Options.OptionText: People have ample time for exercise.
People are aware of health benefits.
Good health tools are costly.
People don't care about exercise.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that individuals value health and understand its importance, motivating them to consider expert advice.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The company decided to merge with a foreign firm.
Assumption: The merger will improve the company’s market position.

Options.OptionText: The foreign firm is financially troubled.
Mergers always result in high profits.
The merger will create operational synergies.
The local firm will lay off employees.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that the merger will harness the strengths of both companies to create synergies that improve market positioning.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The airlines announced a steep hike in airfares.
Assumption: Passengers will still choose to travel by air despite the increase.

Options.OptionText: Air travel is still more convenient than other options.
The airline wants to decrease its passenger load.
Competing airlines will also hike fares.
Passengers have a fixed travel budget.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that despite higher costs, passengers prioritize convenience and time savings, influencing their continued preference for air travel.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The software company decided to offer free online courses.
Assumption: The company wants to enhance customer engagement.

Options.OptionText: Current courses are outdated.
Customers demand more educational content.
Free courses will encourage the use of the company's software.
Online courses enhance technical skills.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that by offering free courses, more users will engage with and subsequently use the company's software, strengthening customer relationships.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Statement: The local council has decided to increase parking fees in the city center.
Assumption: The increase will dissuade excessive car usage.

Options.OptionText: Public transport networks are insufficient.
Carpooling is becoming more popular.
Residents will turn to alternative transport options.
Parking lot revenues have decreased.

m_Item1.Explanation: The assumption is that by increasing parking fees, residents will be encouraged to consider alternative modes of transport, thereby reducing car congestion.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ​
m_Item1.Explanation:
9
Explanation:The number at the centre of each triangle equals the sum of the lower two numbers minus the top number.
